evals.name: workshop-deck
evals.prompt: Create a 6-slide HTML deck for a 30-minute internal workshop titled 'Writing better commit messages'. Audience: junior engineers. Slides should cover: (1) title, (2) why commit messages matter, (3) the conventional commits format, (4) a good vs bad comparison, (5) a 4-step recipe for writing one, (6) wrap with 'Questions?'. Save the file as slides.html in the current working directory.
evals.expected_output: A single self-contained slides.html file with 6 slides matching the established design system

evals.name: feature-readout
evals.prompt: I shipped a feature called 'auto-tagging' that auto-applies labels to incoming support tickets using LLM classification. Make a 7-slide HTML deck for a 5-minute internal demo readout. Slides: (1) title, (2) the problem (manual triage was slow), (3) how it works as an architecture flow (webhook -> classifier -> ticket API), (4) live demo callout, (5) results (3 sample tickets with their auto-applied labels), (6) what's next, (7) wrap. Save as slides.html in the current working directory.
evals.expected_output: slides.html with 7 slides including a title, problem list, architecture flow, demo callout, and wrap

evals.name: doc-to-slides
evals.prompt:
Turn this outline into a 5-slide HTML presentation. Save as slides.html in cwd.

# Q2 Engineering Off-site recap

## What we did
- Half-day on agent infra
- Half-day on shared design-system migration
- Lightning talks (4 of them)

## What landed
- New CI cache layer (30% faster builds)
- Killed 12 unused npm packages
- Agreed: TypeScript strict mode by Q3

## What's next
- Owner assigned to the design-system migration: Maya
- CI cache rollout to remaining repos by end of May
- Strict mode pilot on auth package first

## Audience: full eng team, 10 min readout
evals.expected_output: slides.html with about 5 slides covering title, what we did, what landed, what's next, wrap
